conventions in Eritrea and Ethiopia") * [Ewe](/wiki/Ewe_name "Ewe name") * [Ghanaian](/wiki/Ghanaian_name "Ghana

is the name of the person given upon their birth. The term may be applied to the [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname"), the [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") or to the entire name. Where births are required to be officially registered, the entire name entered onto a births register or [birth certificate](/wiki/Birth_certificate "Birth certificate") may by that fact alone become the person's [legal name](/wiki/Legal_name "Legal name").[[1]](#cite_note-Connexion-1)

(feminine) and **né** (masculine; both pronounced [/neɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Birth_name.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Birth name.wav"); from [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")  *[né[e]](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/n%C3%A9#French "wikt:né")* 'born'), [adopted into English](/wiki/Glossary_of_French_expressions_in_English "Glossary of French expressions in English") from French, have been used to indicate a pre-marital or [maiden name](/wiki/Maiden_and_married_names "Maiden and married names"), or an original birth name that was later changed.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

["French administration must routinely use woman's maiden name in letters"](https://web.archive.org/web/20150923210232/http://www.connexionfrance.com/france-must-use-womans-maiden-name-correspondence-15398-view-article.html). *[The Connexion](/wiki/The_Connexion "The Connexion")*. 27 January 2014. Archived from [the original](http://www.connexionfrance.com/france-must-use-womans-maiden-name-correspondence-15398-view-article.html) on 23 September 2015. Retrieved 1 February 2014. "Laws have existed since the French Revolution stating that 'no citizen can use a first name or surname other than that written on their birth certificate' – but many official organisations address both partners by the husband's surname."

Waddingham, Anne (2014). [*New Hart's Rules: The Oxford Style Guide*](https://books.google.com/books?id=btb1AwAAQBAJ&q=n%C3%A9e+meaning&pg=PA108). [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press"). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780199570027](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780199570027 "Special:BookSources/9780199570027").

["née - definition of née in English from the Oxford dictionary"](https://web.archive.org/web/20140113172135/http://www.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/n%C3%A9e?q=n%C3%A9e). Archived from [the original](http://www.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/n%C3%A9e?q=n%C3%A9e) on 13 January 2014. Retrieved 21 November 2019.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![Scan of birth certificate of Marilyn Monroe](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Marilyn_Monroe_Birth_Certificate_original_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Marilyn_Monroe_Birth_Certificate_original_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marilyn_Monroe_Birth_Certificate_original_(cropped).jpg)

The birth certificate of [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe") displays her name at birth: Norma Jeane Mortenson.

The **birth name** is the name of the person given upon their birth. The term may be applied to the [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname"), the [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") or to the entire name. Where births are required to be officially registered, the entire name entered onto a births register or [birth certificate](/wiki/Birth_certificate "Birth certificate") may by that fact alone become the person's [legal name](/wiki/Legal_name "Legal name").[[1]](#cite_note-Connexion-1)

## Maiden and married names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birth_name&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Maiden and married names")]

Main article: [Maiden and married names](/wiki/Maiden_and_married_names "Maiden and married names")

"De domo" redirects here. For other uses, see [De domo (disambiguation)](/wiki/De_domo_(disambiguation) "De domo (disambiguation)").

The terms **née** (feminine) and **né** (masculine; both pronounced [/neɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Birth_name.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Birth name.wav"); from [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")  *[né[e]](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/n%C3%A9#French "wikt:né")* 'born'), [adopted into English](/wiki/Glossary_of_French_expressions_in_English "Glossary of French expressions in English") from French, have been used to indicate a pre-marital or [maiden name](/wiki/Maiden_and_married_names "Maiden and married names"), or an original birth name that was later changed.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The term *née*, having [feminine grammatical gender](/wiki/Feminine_gender "Feminine gender"), can be used to denote a woman's [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname") at birth that has been replaced or changed. In most English-speaking cultures, it is specifically applied to a woman's maiden name after her surname has changed due to [marriage](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The term *né*, having [masculine grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), can be used to denote a man's surname at birth which has subsequently been replaced or changed. The diacritic marks (the [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent")) are considered significant to its spelling, and ultimately its meaning, but are sometimes omitted.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) According to [Oxford University](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford")'s *[Dictionary of Modern English Usage](/wiki/A_Dictionary_of_Modern_English_Usage "A Dictionary of Modern English Usage")*, the terms are typically placed after the current surname (e.g. 'Ann Smith, *née* Jones' or 'Adam Smith, *né* Jones').[[5]](#cite_note-auto1-5)[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Because they are terms adopted into [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") from French, they do not have to be [italicized](/wiki/Italic_type "Italic type"), but often are.[[5]](#cite_note-auto1-5)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Birth_name&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: See also")]

Archived from [the original](http://www.connexionfrance.com/france-must-use-womans-maiden-name-correspondence-15398-view-article.html) on 23 September 2015. Retrieved 1 February 2014. "Laws have existed since the French Revolution stating that 'no citizen can use a first name or surname other than that written on their birth certificate' – but many official organisations address both partners by the husband's surname."
